title: Chapter 2: Intake
content: The first thing they took was her phone.

Not her dignity — that they'd been chipping at since she was old enough to have opinions. The phone was tactical. A girl without a phone was a girl without an army, and Nurse Holloway knew it.
"Standard protocol," Holloway said, dropping Chloe's iPhone into a labeled Ziploc bag. "You'll earn screen time through progress."
Chloe watched the bag disappear into a drawer. She didn't fight it. Fighting the phone confiscation was what they expected — the addict's withdrawal, the Gen Z panic attack. Instead she catalogued: drawer location, lock type, camera angle.
"Cool," she said. "I was meaning to do a digital detox anyway."
Holloway's smile tightened. Chloe filed that away too: *dislikes when you don't react as predicted.*
The intake room was aggressively beige. Beige walls, beige chairs, beige pamphlets about mindfulness and cognitive restructuring and *your journey to wellness*. On the wall, a framed quote in serif font: "The first step to healing is surrender."
Chloe stared at it until Holloway followed her gaze.
"That's," Chloe said, "the most dystopian thing I've ever seen in a room that wasn't actively on fire."
"It's a therapeutic principle."
"It's a recruitment slogan for a cult."
Holloway made a note on her clipboard. Chloe imagined what it said: *Patient displays oppositional humor. Defensive. Resistant.* She'd read enough psych textbooks to know the playbook. Every reaction she had would be pathologized. Anger = "dysregulation." Silence = "withdrawal." Humor = "deflection."
The trick wasn't to not react.
The trick was to give them reactions they couldn't file.
"So how does this work?" Chloe asked, genuine curiosity bleeding into her voice. "I do enough yoga and you declare me not-crazy?"
"We prefer 'wellness-oriented.' And it's a comprehensive program. Individual therapy, group sessions, medication management, life skills development — "
"Medication management. You mean drugs."
"Pharmaceutical support is a standard component."
"What kind?"
Holloway's pause was a fraction of a second too long. "That's determined by your attending psychiatrist after evaluation."
Translation: *we haven't decided what to give you yet, but you're definitely getting something.*
They assigned her to Room 214.

It was small but not cell-small. A bed with white sheets tucked so tight they looked vacuum-sealed. A window that didn't open. A desk bolted to the floor. A plant — fake, Chloe confirmed by touching its plastic leaves — in a ceramic pot too heavy to lift.
"Dinner's at six," the orderly said. "Lights out at ten. Wake-up at seven. Medication rounds at eight, two, and eight."
"Thrice daily?" Chloe said. "That's ambitious."
"It's protocol."

Chloe sat on the bed. The mattress was firm in a way that felt intentional, like comfort itself was being rationed. Through the wall, she could hear someone crying — the kind of quiet, practiced crying that meant they'd been doing it for a while and had stopped expecting anyone to come.

The orderly left. The door didn't lock from the outside — that would be too obviously a prison. Instead it stayed slightly ajar, monitored by the camera in the corner of the ceiling, its red light blinking like a heartbeat.
Chloe lay back and stared at the camera.
"Hi," she said to it.
The red light blinked. No response. She hadn't expected one.

She did inventory in her head: Phone gone. Laptop gone. No internet. No outside contact. Surveillance 24/7. Mandatory medication. A staff trained to pathologize every reaction. Fellow patients too broken or too scared to be allies.
It was, objectively, a terrible position.

But Chloe had been in terrible positions before. She'd been the only Gen Z intern at a hedge fund run by men who still used fax machines. She'd been doxxed by a men's rights forum with forty thousand members. She'd once had to explain to her father, at Thanksgiving dinner, what "patriarchy" meant.
This? This was just another system.
And Chloe was very, very good at systems.

She closed her eyes and started building a map in her head: the camera placement, the guard rotation, the unlocked door that wasn't really unlocked, the crying girl next door who might be a weakness or might be an asset.
*Step one*, she thought. *Learn the rules.*
*Step two. Break them in ways they can't see.*
Dinner confirmed what the pamphlets had hinted at: this place ran on performance.

The cafeteria was bright and open-plan, all reclaimed wood tables and organic cotton napkins, designed to look good in brochure photos. Patients sat in assigned seats at assigned tables, their trays identically arranged — protein, vegetable, starch, in separate compartments.
Chloe counted seventeen patients. All women. All under thirty. All wearing the same dove-gray loungewear that was one shade too institutional to be actual loungewear.
"New girl," said the orderly escorting her. "Table Four."
Table Four had three other women. None of them looked up when Chloe sat down.

The one directly across from her was maybe nineteen, Korean-American, with wire-rimmed glasses and fingers that tapped against her thigh even when she wasn't holding anything. Coding fingers. Chloe recognized the rhythm.
The one to Chloe's left was blonde, model-thin, and eating her peas one at a time with surgical precision. Her posture was so perfect it looked painful.
The one to Chloe's right was Black, built like a track athlete, and staring at her tray like it had personally offended her.
"Hi," Chloe said. "I'm Chloe. I just got committed by my mother for posting a TikTok that made my dad's board of directors uncomfortable."

Track Athlete snorted without looking up. "At least yours had a reason. Mine put me here because I 'wasn't acting like a proper young lady.' Which is what happens when you win state championships and don't smile enough."
"Maya," she added, after a beat. "Shot put. Two-time California state champion."
"My mother thinks I have an eating disorder," Blonde said, her voice barely above a whisper. "I don't. I just didn't want to marry a forty-seven-year-old hedge fund manager." She paused. "Sophia."
Chloe looked at the girl with the coding fingers. She was the only one who hadn't spoken.
"And you?" Chloe asked.
The girl's fingers stopped tapping. She looked up — not at Chloe's face, but at the camera in the corner of the ceiling.
"I'm here," she said quietly, "because I wouldn't build my father a surveillance system."
Chloe felt something click into place. Like a lock she hadn't known was there.
"What's your name?" she asked.
"Layla."
Chloe smiled. Not the performative smile she'd given Nurse Holloway. A real one. The kind that said *I see you. You see me. This changes things.*
"Layla," she said. "Tell me everything you know about those cameras."
Layla's eyes flicked to the ceiling, then back to Chloe. Her fingers resumed their tapping — but slower now. Deliberate.
"Every hallway," she said. "Every common room. Every patient room except the bathrooms. They say it's for safety. They watch the feeds from a control room on the second floor."
"And you know this how?"
Layla's mouth quirked — not quite a smile. "I may have been committed for refusing to build surveillance. That doesn't mean I don't know how it works."
Chloe leaned forward. "Can you get into it?"
A long pause. Layla's fingers stopped.
"I'd need a device," she said. "Anything with a processor and a network stack. They took everything I had, but..."
"But?"
"There's a supply closet on the ground floor. Old equipment. Things they forgot about." Layla met Chloe's eyes. "If someone could get in there."
Chloe's smile widened. "Bestie," she said, "I think this is the beginning of a beautiful friendship."
Eight o'clock came. Medication rounds.
A different orderly — younger, male, with the dead-eyed look of someone who'd taken this job for the student loan forgiveness — arrived at Chloe's door with a paper cup.
"Evening meds," he said.

Chloe took the cup. Two pills: one white, one pale blue. She'd done her research. White was probably an SSRI — Paxil or Zoloft, the starter pack for "difficult" women. Pale blue was likely a mood stabilizer. Lamictal, maybe. Something to flatten her out.
"Open your mouth after," the orderly said. "Tongue check."
Standard protocol. They knew patients tried to cheek pills.
Chloe put the pills in her mouth. Took the water. Swallowed — visibly, throat moving. Opened her mouth. Lifted her tongue.
The orderly nodded and moved on.
Chloe waited until his footsteps faded. Then she reached under her tongue, where she'd pinned both pills against the frenulum, undetectable to anyone who didn't know exactly where to look.
She pulled them out. Dropped them into the hollowed-out spine of the fake plant on her desk.
*Step one*, she thought. *Learn the rules.*
*Step two.*
She smiled at the camera.
*Break them in ways they can't see.*
